inline_review:
commit e2b67f9305111ab74d9a84d03364af73cfb7ea77
Author: Deepanshu Kartikey <kartikey406@gmail.com>

    mm/userfaultfd: re-validate vma in mfill_atomic() loop under CONFIG_PER_VMA_LOCK

The commit adds a mid-loop VMA bounds check to mfill_atomic() to handle
concurrent UFFDIO_UNREGISTER splits. It re-acquires the VMA if the current
address falls outside the split VMA's bounds.

Reported-by: syzbot+e24a2e34fad0efbac047@syzkaller.appspotmail.com
Closes: https://syzkaller.appspot.com/bug?extid=e24a2e34fad0efbac047
Tested-by: syzbot+e24a2e34fad0efbac047@syzkaller.appspotmail.com

> diff --git a/mm/userfaultfd.c b/mm/userfaultfd.c
> index 510c6dcb9824..afb3c932d470 100644
> --- a/mm/userfaultfd.c
> +++ b/mm/userfaultfd.c
> @@ -910,6 +910,22 @@ static __always_inline ssize_t mfill_atomic(struct userfaultfd_ctx *ctx,
>  
>  	while (state.src_addr < src_start + len) {
>  		VM_WARN_ON_ONCE(state.dst_addr >= dst_start + len);
> +	       /*
> +		* Under CONFIG_PER_VMA_LOCK, a concurrent UFFDIO_UNREGISTER can
> +		* split state.vma while we hold only the per-VMA read lock. The
> +		* split shrinks vma->vm_end in place, causing dst_addr to fall
> +		* outside the VMA bounds. Re-validate dst_addr on each iteration
> +		* and re-lookup the vma if it has been split.
> +		*/
> +		if (state.dst_addr < state.vma->vm_start ||
> +		    state.dst_addr >= state.vma->vm_end) {
> +			mfill_put_vma(&state);
> +			state.dst_start = state.dst_addr;
> +			state.len = dst_start + len - state.dst_addr;

Does the omission of the .len field in the initial designated initializer
for state cause state.len to be initialized to 0 at the start of
mfill_atomic()?

If so, the first call to mfill_get_vma(&state) outside this loop passes
state.len (0) to uffd_mfill_lock(), bypassing the requirement that the
entire requested range fits within the VMA. While the new mid-loop check
masks the resulting out-of-bounds access, it appears cross-VMA operations
are improperly allowed to begin processing.

> +			err = mfill_get_vma(&state);

Can this lead to a lock leak on error paths?

mfill_put_vma(&state) sets state.vma = NULL before this call. Inside
mfill_get_vma(), a new dst_vma is locked and map_changing_lock is acquired.

If validation fails (for example, !ops because the VMA was split by
UFFDIO_UNREGISTER), mfill_get_vma() jumps to out_unlock and calls
mfill_put_vma(state).

Since state->vma is only updated to dst_vma on success at the out label,
it is still NULL during the error path. mfill_put_vma() will immediately
return, failing to release map_changing_lock and unlock the VMA.

Can this also lead to page table corruption if the re-acquired VMA is a
hugetlb VMA?

mfill_get_vma() allows hugetlb VMAs, returning 0 and skipping ops checks
via the is_vm_hugetlb_page(dst_vma) branch.

If a concurrent thread replaces the normal VMA at dst_addr with a hugetlb
VMA, mfill_get_vma() will succeed and update state.vma. The loop will then
continue and call mfill_get_pmd() and mfill_atomic_pte(), which allocate
standard PMDs and PTEs for a hugetlb mapping.

> +			if (err)
> +				break;
> +		}
>  
>  		err = mfill_get_pmd(&state);

Does mfill_atomic_hugetlb() require a similar fix?

It holds the same uffd_mfill_lock() across its loop but lacks this new
in-loop bounds re-validation check against dst_vma->vm_end.

If the hugetlb VMA shrinks concurrently via UFFDIO_UNREGISTER, dst_addr
could exceed the VMA bounds, causing hugetlb_mfill_atomic_pte() to be
called with an out-of-bounds address.

Target Commit:
commit e2b67f9305111ab74d9a84d03364af73cfb7ea77
Author: Deepanshu Kartikey <kartikey406@gmail.com>
Date:   Mon Mar 16 07:00:39 2026 +0000

    mm/userfaultfd: re-validate vma in mfill_atomic() loop under CONFIG_PER_VMA_LOCK
    
    Under CONFIG_PER_VMA_LOCK, mfill_atomic() holds only a per-VMA read
    lock across its page-by-page copy loop. A concurrent UFFDIO_UNREGISTER
    can acquire mmap_write_lock() and split the VMA mid-loop via
    __split_vma(), which calls vma_start_write() via __vma_enter_locked().
    
    The split happens in the race window between CHECK 1 and vm_refcnt++
    in vma_start_read(). During this window vm_refcnt equals the base
    attached value, so vma_start_write() sees no readers and proceeds
    immediately without waiting, shrinking vma->vm_end in place.
    
    Both seqnum checks in vma_start_read() miss this because after
    mmap_write_unlock(), mm_lock_seq is incremented past vm_lock_seq
    making them unequal, so a split VMA is returned to mfill_atomic().
    
    On the next iteration, mfill_atomic_install_pte() calls
    folio_add_new_anon_rmap() with state.dst_addr >= vma->vm_end,
    triggering its sanity check:
    
      address < vma->vm_start || address + (nr << 12) > vma->vm_end
      WARNING: mm/rmap.c:1682 folio_add_new_anon_rmap+0x5fe/0x14b0
    
    Fix this by checking on each loop iteration whether state.dst_addr
    has fallen outside state.vma. If so, release the stale vma, update
    dst_start and len to reflect the current position, and re-lookup the
    vma via mfill_get_vma().
    
    Reported-by: syzbot+e24a2e34fad0efbac047@syzkaller.appspotmail.com
    Closes: https://syzkaller.appspot.com/bug?extid=e24a2e34fad0efbac047"
    Tested-by: syzbot+e24a2e34fad0efbac047@syzkaller.appspotmail.com
    Signed-off-by: Deepanshu Kartikey <Kartikey406@gmail.com>

diff --git a/mm/userfaultfd.c b/mm/userfaultfd.c
index 510c6dcb9824..afb3c932d470 100644
--- a/mm/userfaultfd.c
+++ b/mm/userfaultfd.c
@@ -910,6 +910,22 @@ static __always_inline ssize_t mfill_atomic(struct userfaultfd_ctx *ctx,
 
 	while (state.src_addr < src_start + len) {
 		VM_WARN_ON_ONCE(state.dst_addr >= dst_start + len);
+	       /*
+		* Under CONFIG_PER_VMA_LOCK, a concurrent UFFDIO_UNREGISTER can
+		* split state.vma while we hold only the per-VMA read lock. The
+		* split shrinks vma->vm_end in place, causing dst_addr to fall
+		* outside the VMA bounds. Re-validate dst_addr on each iteration
+		* and re-lookup the vma if it has been split.
+		*/
+		if (state.dst_addr < state.vma->vm_start ||
+		    state.dst_addr >= state.vma->vm_end) {
+			mfill_put_vma(&state);
+			state.dst_start = state.dst_addr;
+			state.len = dst_start + len - state.dst_addr;
+			err = mfill_get_vma(&state);
+			if (err)
+				break;
+		}
 
 		err = mfill_get_pmd(&state);
 		if (err)

    "thought": "**Analyzing the Userfaultfd VMA Revalidation Fix**

Okay, so the commit message explains a potential race condition in `mfill_atomic` when `CONFIG_PER_VMA_LOCK` is enabled, specifically concerning userfaultfd and VMA splitting. My initial thought is that with the per-VMA read lock held by `mfill_atomic`, how *can* a concurrent `UFFDIO_UNREGISTER` split the VMA? The commit clarifies a specific window: during the acquisition of the VMA read lock. `vma_start_write` might sneak in and modify the VMA *while* the reader is acquiring the read lock.

The sequence check in `vma_start_read()` somehow misses this race, resulting in `mfill_atomic` getting a VMA that's been shrunk or possibly freed. This is where the issue lies. If the writer shrinks the VMA's `vm_end`, the subsequent page copy in `mfill_atomic` can lead to problems. The commit suggests revalidating the VMA within the `mfill_atomic` loop. The provided patch does this by checking if the current destination address (`state.dst_addr`) has fallen outside the VMA bounds and re-acquiring the VMA, which is a sensible approach.

I checked how the VMA locking mechanisms work to gain a deeper understanding. I was initially concerned about how the stale `dst_start` and `len` might be used during the re-lookup. However, after careful examination, I'm confident that these values are correctly updated using the original values from the outer function, ensuring the correct remaining length is calculated. I verified that `mfill_get_vma` uses the updated `state.dst_start` and `state.len` during the VMA lookup, which confirms my understanding.

I then revisited the commit message's claim about `vma_start_read` missing the race. If `vma_start_read` successfully acquires the lock, then there is a fundamental issue! However, the `vma_start_read` has the sequence check to prevent race conditions. The root cause appears to be the race condition, *not* a flaw in the locking mechanisms, which is clarified by the explanation that the split happens during the lock acquisition. If the VMA shrinks *after* `vma_start_read`, `mfill_atomic` gets a potentially invalid VMA. I checked the locking functions (like `lock_vma_under_rcu()`) and confirmed that they re-validate the VMA bounds after acquiring the lock and before using the address, so a stale VMA should be detected.

In short, it looks like this patch provides a reasonable workaround for the race condition, by re-validating the VMA within the loop of `mfill_atomic` and re-acquiring it when necessary.

    "thought": "**Analyzing the VMA Read Lock Issue**

Okay, so I'm looking at this patch and, at first glance, the fix seems straightforward. We check if `state.dst_addr` has fallen outside `state.vma` in each loop iteration, release the stale VMA, update the start and length, and re-lookup the VMA. But, wait a minute, `mfill_put_vma` drops the VMA read lock. Then `mfill_get_vma` reacquires it.

    "thought": "**Analyzing a Potential VMA Split Race Condition**

Okay, so I'm looking at this commit and the explanation is, as usual, dense. The core issue seems to be a race condition involving a VMA split that occurs within a specific window between `CHECK 1` and the increment of `vm_refcnt` in `vma_start_read()`. The commit message states that during this race, `vm_refcnt` is equal to the base attached value. This allows `vma_start_write()` to proceed without waiting, immediately shrinking `vma->vm_end`.

The concern is that if a VMA is split mid-operation, `mfill_atomic` will loop, and then `mfill_atomic_install_pte` *might* try to use `dst_addr` outside the now-shrunk `vma->vm_end`. The author is attempting to fix this with a check:

```c
		if (state.dst_addr < state.vma->vm_start ||
		    state.dst_addr >= state.vma->vm_end) {
			mfill_put_vma(&state);
			state.dst_start = state.dst_addr;
			state.len = dst_start + len - state.dst_addr;
			err = mfill_get_vma(&state);
			if (err)
				break;
		}
```

My initial thought is, is this safe? I'm trying to wrap my head around the scenario. If the VMA *was* split and modified while a read lock was *supposedly* held, how is that possible? Ah, the race condition in `vma_start_read()`... if that race truly exists, the read lock *isn't* actually protecting the VMA because of the race!

If `vma_start_read` successfully returns, the caller *thinks* it has a read lock, but the VMA was already shrunk and the writer has unlocked. Crucially, if the race occurs, `vma_start_read` returns *with* the `vma->vm_refcnt` incremented, but the writer had *already* completed and unlocked. The writer is gone! Effectively, the reader now has a read lock on the shrunk VMA, not the original.

Okay, so now that I get the crux, if the reader holds a read lock on the *shrunk* VMA, nothing else can shrink it further without waiting.  But that means `lock_vma_under_rcu` is going to succeed if the VMA was split and `dst_start` is still *inside* the VMA, which it must be because of the fix in question!

However, the end of the VMA could be `dst_start + 1 * PAGE_SIZE`, while `state.len` is *much* larger! `mfill_atomic` loops over the range `[dst_start, dst_start + len)`. The crucial point is `uffd_mfill_lock` which does `validate_dst_vma` which should check the bounds. 

I see the light! `validate_dst_vma` should check `dst_start + len <= dst_vma->vm_end`. If the VMA *was* shrunk, `validate_dst_vma` would return `FALSE` because `dst_start + len` would now be larger than the new smaller `vm_end`. So, this new check *should* fix the issue by correcting the VMA range before installation. I'm going to quickly check `validate_dst_vma`'s implementation to confirm this final point...

    "thought": "**Analyzing the Userfaultfd VMA Validation and Potential Race Condition**

Okay, let's break this down. I'm looking at the `validate_dst_vma` function, specifically how it's used in the `mfill_atomic` process within the userfaultfd implementation. The core of the issue seems to be around VMA validation when VMAs are potentially split during the operation, particularly due to `UFFDIO_UNREGISTER`.

The initial check in `validate_dst_vma` is straightforward: ensure the destination range is fully contained within the VMA. In `mfill_atomic`, there's a loop that aims to fill memory.  If the destination address falls outside the current VMA, the code retrieves a new VMA using `mfill_get_vma`. This function, crucially, calls `uffd_mfill_lock`, which in turn, calls `validate_dst_vma`.

Here's where the problem arises: If a VMA is split *after* an initial VMA lookup but *before* the full write operation, `validate_dst_vma` might be called with the wrong parameters. The `state.len` represents the remaining length of the entire operation, which can exceed the newly split VMA's end boundary if the split happens to a smaller VMA. This would cause the validation to fail, and the `mfill_atomic` operation would prematurely abort with `-ENOENT`. This also applies if `UFFDIO_UNREGISTER` splits the VMA; the unregistered part is now invalid.

The `mfill_atomic` function's design assumes the entire fill range is within a single VMA. But, if `UFFDIO_UNREGISTER` splits the VMA, a subsequent check will fail because the VMA (or part of it) will be no longer registered. That's the correct behavior, because unregistered memory shouldn't be filled!

But let's consider a scenario where a split happens for *other* reasons.  `mfill_atomic`'s short-return behavior is correct if `mfill_get_vma` fails.

However, the question is how the VMA split can occur *during* a running `mfill_atomic` loop. If `UFFDIO_UNREGISTER` splits the VMA *while* the fill is in progress, the timing is critical. The commit message mentions a race condition between `CHECK 1` and `vm_refcnt++` in `vma_start_read()`. This suggests the split happens *before* even grabbing a read lock on the VMA.

The critical issue is this: If `vma_start_read` succeeds (grabs the read lock), but a split happens immediately after, `mfill_get_vma` will still call `validate_dst_vma`, but the VMA will now be smaller, and the `dst_start + len` calculation will be larger than the new `vm_end`, and `validate_dst_vma` will correctly return false.

The sequence: find the VMA -> writer splits the VMA -> grab read lock -> check `vma->vm_start <= addr && vma->vm_end > addr` (passes because the read lock got the old VMA end boundary) -> call `validate_dst_vma` (fails because new VMA's `vm_end` is smaller, and `dst_start + len` is the remaining original size) -> `mfill_get_vma` fails -> `-ENOENT` short-return.

The commit message seems to indicate a discrepancy, stating that the split VMA is returned. This suggests that the validation *somehow* passed. But if the VMA *is* split AFTER `validate_dst_vma` passes, it means that the split happened *while* `mfill_atomic` had a read lock on the VMA.

Here's the problem: The VMA *shouldn't* be splittable while `mfill_atomic` holds a read lock on it. The `vm_refcnt` should be greater than zero. Therefore the writer cannot call `vma_start_write` to split the VMA while there is an active reader!

Going to trace `split_vma` in `mm/vma.c` to understand where `vma_start_write` is called!

The primary issue I'm seeing is a potential lock leak in `mfill_get_vma`. Line 263, specifically, is a hotspot. It calls `mfill_put_vma(state)` right before returning from a function call with an error code, which might cause the `state->vma` to be NULL and the locks `dst_vma` and `ctx->map_changing_lock` to be leaked. This happens because `mfill_put_vma` skips the unlock operation if the `state->vma` is `NULL`.

The root cause of this is that `state->vma` is assigned *after* the `uffd_mfill_lock` and `down_read` calls, which acquire the locks. If *any* error condition happens before setting `state->vma`, like, say, the `ctx->mmap_changing` flag is set, the function jumps to `out_unlock`, where it calls `mfill_put_vma` with a `NULL` `state->vma`, leading to the leak of those locks. The current patch introduces a loop calling `mfill_get_vma`, and in it, `state->vma` is initialized to NULL with `mfill_put_vma(&state)`. The error cases are triggered often. This pre-existing bug has been exposed by the introduction of the loop, since `mfill_get_vma` is called repeatedly.

Furthermore, there are concerns about the interaction between regular and hugetlb VMAs. The patch’s logic, when encountering a VMA which happens to be a hugetlb VMA, it can erroneously call functions like `mfill_get_pmd` and `mfill_atomic_pte` on it, potentially leading to corruption or crashes. The code seems to assume a standard page table structure, while a hugetlb VMA has a different page table structure. This happens because the existing structure and code flow of `mfill_atomic` will jump into the loop if the initial VMA is *not* a hugetlb, but there is nothing to prevent the situation in which the hugetlb VMA is introduced during the looping process. It's theoretically possible for a hugetlb VMA to be mapped and registered with the same userfaultfd context as the original one, and in that circumstance, it might trigger the error if there are races happening, as `mmap_write_lock` is not held in that flow.

Ultimately, I'm finding that the most immediate concern is the lock leak in `mfill_get_vma` and that this patch is introducing a loop that may trigger the leak more frequently than it may have previously, so it’s worth highlighting. I need to flag it as a risk.

    "thought": "**Critical Analysis of Userfaultfd Patch**

Okay, here’s the breakdown of my thinking on this patch. I've uncovered some pretty serious issues here, so let's walk through it.

First, I spotted that `mfill_copy_folio_retry` is calling `mfill_get_vma` multiple times, which immediately set off alarm bells. My worry was that repeated calls with an error return could potentially leak locks.

After carefully tracing `mfill_get_vma`, I confirmed the worst. The function has a lock, but when it encounters certain error conditions, the `mfill_put_vma` function is not always executed. In particular, if `atomic_read(&ctx->mmap_changing)` is true (due to concurrent operations like `mremap` or `mprotect`), it jumps to a point where `state->vma` is still `NULL`, so `mfill_put_vma` does *nothing* and returns an error! This leaves the VMA lock and `map_changing_lock` leaked, which is a major, and very reproducible lock leak! This is a severe, and potentially critical, bug that I need to report.

I also saw that the patch introduces a significant hugetlb vulnerability. The `mfill_get_vma` function returns 0 (success) if the new VMA is a hugetlb VMA. While this is expected on the initial call, the patch *adds* a call to `mfill_get_vma` inside a loop. If a normal VMA is replaced with a hugetlb VMA *during* the copy, and the loop calls `mfill_get_vma` after the swap, this causes a dangerous type confusion. The code then proceeds to call `mfill_get_pmd` on what is now a hugetlb address, which is completely wrong and corrupts the hugetlb page tables. This is another serious issue that needs immediate attention.

I then investigated what happens when the VMA is split by other means, such as `mprotect` or `UFFDIO_UNREGISTER`. If `UFFDIO_UNREGISTER` splits the VMA, the loop's `mfill_get_vma` will return `-ENOENT` and abort, which, while correct and expected, is the *only* behavior, and userspace *must* be prepared.

If `mprotect` is used, the logic seems to work as intended *unless* the `mprotect` split creates a protected VMA that *doesn't* extend to the original end. In that case, `validate_dst_vma` fails because the remaining region is now outside the protected VMA. This also causes a `-ENOENT` return. In this case, while it is valid behavior, userspace must deal with it.

Finally, I checked all potential race conditions and other issues introduced by the patch. All checks on the `state` were performed correctly, and the patch, while containing critical bugs, does not seem to contain further logic errors, except for the missing check.

So, to summarize, this patch introduces two critical bugs and a minor (but important) point about the documented behavior of `UFFDIO_COPY` with split VMAs. The first major issue is a significant lock leak that is trivially reproducible, and the second is a type confusion that can lead to hugetlb page table corruption. I need to report these issues immediately.

Okay, so let me break this down. My initial thought process is centered around how `mfill_copy_folio_retry` interacts with the VMA and the potential for lock leaks or race conditions.

First, the code itself seems fine, using `mfill_put_vma` and `mfill_get_vma` correctly, which should update the range as we fill memory.

However, a critical concern pops up: what if `mfill_get_vma` fails within `mfill_copy_folio_retry`? This could lead to an immediate lock leak due to the sequence of `mfill_put_vma` being called *before* `mfill_get_vma`. If the latter fails, the VMA lock and `map_changing_lock` are leaked. This suggests a pre-existing flaw in `mfill_get_vma` that the patch merely exposes more frequently. I should flag this as a potential side effect.

Next, I'm diving into the potential race with VMA splits, which is the crux of this problem. My initial thought is about the safety of `state.vma->vm_start` read. The per-VMA read lock *should* protect `vm_start` and `vm_end` from being changed concurrently, but the commit message raises flags, so I am going to investigate.

The commit message claims a VMA split can occur during a concurrent `UFFDIO_UNREGISTER`, shrinking `vma->vm_end`. The claim is the split is possible because `vma_start_write()` doesn't block properly. This is counter-intuitive. `vma_start_read` uses `vm_refcnt` to track readers, and a writer is *supposed* to wait, but the commit message suggests there's a window where the writer can slip through.

If `mfill_atomic` already holds the VMA lock and is in a loop, it means it *has* passed the checks in `vma_start_read()`, so `vm_refcnt` should be incremented. Therefore, `vma_start_write()` should block. The logic and the commit message seem to disagree here. I'm going to follow the logic through and see what is actually happening.

I see that `vma_start_read()` uses a sequence check with `mm_lock_seq` to determine if a write is in progress. The commit suggests this sequence check can miss the VMA split. If the entire write (VMA split) occurs within the read race window, the sequence check in `vma_start_read()` may return the *split* VMA.

The critical issue is the `validate_dst_vma` check within `uffd_mfill_lock()`. `validate_dst_vma` checks `dst_end > dst_vma->vm_end`, and I realize that if the VMA was split during `vma_start_read`, `dst_vma->vm_end` will have already shrunk *before* `uffd_mfill_lock` calls this.

This causes `validate_dst_vma` to return `false`, which leads to `uffd_mfill_lock` returning `ERR_PTR(-ENOENT)`. Thus, the loop *never* sees a split VMA. In fact, if the VMA gets split, the entire operation fails *before* it even begins.

So where does the "dst_addr >= vma->vm_end inside the loop" come from? My thought is, what if the VMA split occurs in `mfill_copy_folio_retry()` *after* it releases the lock, but before it reacquires? This would allow the race to occur within the called function, and cause `validate_dst_vma` to fail.

Then I double-check the lock implementation, and I realize `vma_start_read()` *does* hold the VMA lock using a read lock (`down_read_trylock`) and that `vma_start_write()` is designed to block on this (using `down_write`). The commit message's description of the race appears incorrect; the writer *should* be blocked. This is key. The comment claims the problem is a missing per-VMA lock but the per-VMA lock *is* being used!

I'm starting to think the commit author may be mistaken about the root cause and that the sequence count check and the rwsem locks used in the VMA read/write are interacting in unexpected ways.     "thought": "**Analyzing the Potential Race Condition in `mfill_atomic`**

Okay, so I've been digging into this `mfill_atomic` function and I think I've spotted a significant vulnerability, particularly when dealing with concurrent `UFFDIO_UNREGISTER` and `mmap` calls.  It boils down to the fact that the code doesn't reliably handle a VMA's type changing underneath it during a copy operation.

The core of the issue stems from `mfill_get_vma` being called within the `mfill_atomic` loop, and the fact that a concurrent process *could* replace a regular anonymous or shared memory VMA with a hugetlb VMA *after* the initial check and lock but *before* subsequent PMD/PTE manipulations.  The critical path I've identified is this:

1.  We start with a non-hugetlb VMA, *and* have acquired a lock on it with `uffd_mfill_lock`.
2.  The loop in `mfill_atomic` proceeds.
3.  A concurrent thread calls `UFFDIO_UNREGISTER` to unmap a section of the VMA, which potentially splits the VMA.
4.  The concurrent thread *then* calls `mmap` with `MAP_HUGETLB` on the exact same address range that was just unmapped, AND registers it with userfaultfd using the *same context*.
5.  `mfill_atomic` now re-enters the loop, which calls `mfill_get_vma`.
6.  `mfill_get_vma` finds the *new* hugetlb VMA (because it's registered with userfaultfd and within bounds), returns *without* error, and sets `state.vma` to the hugetlb VMA.
7.  The loop continues into `mfill_get_pmd`, *and* then `mfill_atomic_pte` is called.

The problem here is that `mfill_get_pmd` and `mfill_atomic_pte` are *not* designed to work with hugetlb VMAs!  `mm_alloc_pmd` is called on a hugetlb VMA address which is *not* what's supposed to happen, and regular PTE allocation functions are invoked on what is now a hugetlb VMA. This is extremely likely to lead to corruption or a crash. This race also holds if we're switching between anon/shmem/hugetlb.

The original code *did* have a check (`is_vm_hugetlb_page(state.vma)`) *before* the loop, but it's now bypassed due to the re-lookup in `mfill_get_vma` inside the loop. The patch introduces this new `mfill_get_vma` lookup that doesn't check for this specific VMA type change. The original check would delegate to `mfill_atomic_hugetlb`, but this path is now broken.

My first thought is that the fix is to *check* for the hugetlb VMA again inside the loop, *after* re-acquiring the VMA with `mfill_get_vma`.  If it's a hugetlb VMA at that point, we return an error (`-ENOENT` or `-EINVAL`) rather than proceeding with normal PTE operations.  We also need to remember that `validate_dst_vma` requires the VMA to be registered with userfaultfd, so in order to be valid, the concurrent thread would also have to register the new hugetlb VMA with userfaultfd.

There's another critical problem if `mfill_get_vma` fails. Let's look at that path again: if `mfill_get_vma` fails, it calls `mfill_put_vma` from the `out_unlock` path. Inside `mfill_put_vma`, the code unlocks the VMA. But `mfill_get_vma` *never* sets `state.vma` to a valid VMA if the return is from `out_unlock`. This means we are *leaking* a read lock! `down_read(&ctx->map_changing_lock)` is never unlocked.

So, the original code had a subtle race which is now a vulnerability if you unmap, map hugetlb, and register under our feet. I'm thinking the correct fix is to return an error from `mfill_get_vma` if it encounters a VMA that is a different type than it expects. Additionally, the lock leak in `out_unlock` needs to be addressed.

If any of those checks fail, we're supposed to jump to `out_unlock`.  The intent is clear: release the acquired locks and return an error.

The problem lies in how `out_unlock` is handled.  `mfill_put_vma` is called, but that function checks `state->vma`. Now, `state->vma` is only assigned *inside* `mfill_get_vma` at the `out:` label, *after* the checks.  If any of those checks fail and the function jumps to `out_unlock`, the `state->vma` variable will be `NULL`.

So, here's the scenario: if any of those checks in `mfill_get_vma` fail, `mfill_put_vma` is called, sees that `state->vma` is `NULL`, and simply returns. 2.  `down_read(&ctx->map_changing_lock)` succeeds and acquires the lock.

9. The calling function catches the error.
10. `map_changing_lock` is leaked because the read lock was acquired but not released.
11. VMA lock is leaked because the VMA lock was acquired but not released.

    "thought": "**Analysis of Lock Management and Hugetlb Interaction in Userfaultfd Patch**

Alright, after meticulously reviewing the code, several critical issues have come to light. The patch introduces significant problems related to lock management and interaction with hugetlb pages, exacerbating pre-existing but previously less critical issues.

First and foremost, the error handling within `mfill_get_vma` is fundamentally flawed. If `mfill_get_vma` encounters an error during VMA lookup and validation, it jumps to `out_unlock`. However, the critical `state->vma` pointer, which holds the acquired VMA, is only assigned *after* the `out:` label. Consequently, `mfill_put_vma` is called with `state->vma` being `NULL`, leading to a double lock leak. Specifically, the read lock on `ctx->map_changing_lock` and the VMA lock acquired by `uffd_mfill_lock` on `dst_vma` are not released, resulting in resource exhaustion and potential system instability. This is an existing bug, and the patch, by introducing a new call to `mfill_get_vma` inside the loop, amplifies the issue.

The patch's modification also causes a particularly insidious bug with hugetlb pages. If, within the loop, the current VMA gets replaced by a hugetlb VMA concurrently, the original code flow bypasses the check intended for hugetlb VMAs (specifically, the early return in `mfill_atomic` based on `is_vm_hugetlb_page`). The loop continues to `mfill_get_pmd` and `mfill_atomic_pte`, which will then allocate standard page table entries for what is now a hugetlb VMA, corrupting the page tables and very likely causing a kernel panic. The original code did not suffer from this issue.

Another significant issue relates to the update of `state.dst_start` and `state.len` within the loop. The code updates these parameters to look up the new VMA to process, based on the current `dst_addr`. While it appears the logic is correct, there is an issue to the fact that `.len` in struct `mfill_state` is uninitialized. This may be benign, but it is incorrect and must be addressed. Further, this patch may cause issues when VMAs are split across an operation. While in this case, it is correct that `mfill_get_vma` fails (as we expect operations to be fully inside a single VMA), the original issue is that the initial `len` parameter is not initialized in the struct `mfill_state`.

In essence, these findings highlight a significant weakness in the error-handling paradigm in `mfill_get_vma`. The patch, while not directly causing the root cause (the lock leak), introduces a new usage scenario that exacerbates it, and introduces a new issue. These vulnerabilities have the potential to introduce lock leaks, page table corruption, and potential system crashes.

I must report this because "Do not be overly charitable to the existing code."

    "thought": "**Analyzing the Userfaultfd `mfill_atomic` Vulnerability**

Okay, I'm diving back into this `mfill_atomic` function, and the initialization immediately jumps out at me: `state.len` is set to... nothing. It's just left at zero! How could that be? The struct definition clearly includes `len`. I immediately check: Is `state.len` ever assigned before the crucial `mfill_get_vma` call? Nope! This means the initial validation of the destination VMA, via `uffd_mfill_lock` and `validate_dst_vma`, only checks that `dst_start` falls within the VMA's bounds, because the `len` argument is zero!

The implications are suddenly stark. The original code *didn't* validate that the *entire* requested length fits within a single VMA; it only checked the starting address! This means an operation could have spanned multiple VMAs, a blatant error. And then I remember the loop in `mfill_atomic`: `while (state.src_addr < src_start + len)`. `dst_addr` increments by `PAGE_SIZE` with each iteration. But the code *never* re-checks `vma->vm_end` within that loop!

The code would allocate PMDs and PTEs in the *next* VMA but crucially, it would still use the flags and protection attributes (`dst_vma->vm_flags` and `dst_vma->vm_page_prot`) from the *first* VMA. This is a severe security issue.

So, if the caller of `mfill_atomic` passes a `len` that spans multiple VMAs, `mfill_atomic` would lock the first VMA but then, because of the missing length validation, it would proceed to blindly write to the second, third, etc. VMAs.

Wait, the patch… aha! The patch adds a critical check: `state.dst_addr >= state.vma->vm_end`. Before this, there was NO validation! Now, if `len` does span multiple VMAs, the `state.dst_addr` will eventually exceed the `vma->vm_end`, triggering the check! Crucially, the patch *also* updates `state.len` with the *remaining* length: `state.len = dst_start + len - state.dst_addr`.

This is the key. When `mfill_get_vma` is called again after the VMA boundary is crossed, now, `state.len` is the remaining length. And now, `uffd_mfill_lock` is called with the *remaining length*, and `validate_dst_vma` correctly checks that the `dst_end` is within the second VMA's bounds! If the original range (the supplied `len`) still spans VMAs, the check will fail.

So, this patch isn't just fixing a bug, it's changing the *behavior*. Before, it might have allowed a cross-VMA operation to *partially* succeed or perhaps cause other unintended consequences. The patch now introduces a clear and correct failure with `-ENOENT` when a requested range spans multiple VMAs.

I need to confirm if I really did understand the initial problem, let me double check `mfill_atomic` again.

Okay, let's break this down. I'm focusing on `mfill_get_vma` and its interaction with the `mfill_put_vma` function, specifically looking for any potential lock leaks. The initial observation is that if `mfill_get_vma` fails and jumps to `out_unlock`, the `state->vma` pointer isn't set. Consequently, `mfill_put_vma` will return early because it checks for a null `state->vma`. This implies that `ctx->map_changing_lock` and the memory region locked via `uffd_mfill_lock` (`dst_vma`) are *not* unlocked in the failure path.

I'm verifying that my initial understanding is accurate by examining the code more closely. It looks like the original code structure has the assignment of `state->vma = dst_vma` within the `out:` label, and that the `out_unlock:` label simply calls `mfill_put_vma(state)`. Since `state->vma` is only set in the success path, the failure path would indeed have `state->vma` as NULL.

A review of the implementation of `mfill_put_vma` confirms that if `state->vma` is NULL, it returns immediately, leaving the read lock on `ctx->map_changing_lock` and the `uffd_mfill_lock` on the `dst_vma` still held.

Therefore, *the potential for a lock leak is significant in the current code*. A failed call to `mfill_get_vma` will not unlock the resources it acquires.

Now, let's consider the context of the patch. The patch does not modify `mfill_get_vma` itself, which means this bug is *pre-existing*. However, the patch *does* call `mfill_get_vma` within the new `mfill_atomic` function. I'm examining this specific path now. In `mfill_atomic`, if the destination address is outside the current VMA range, we call `mfill_put_vma` (which *explicitly* sets `state.vma` to NULL) and then call `mfill_get_vma`. If `mfill_get_vma` fails in this context, it will call `mfill_put_vma` again (which does nothing), and we'll break out of the loop. Crucially, the outer function *also* calls `mfill_put_vma` after the loop. Since this second call is after the potential failure in `mfill_get_vma`, it *also* does nothing, and the lock leak persists. Thus, the new `mfill_atomic` function will also have the potential for a lock leak if `mfill_get_vma` fails.

I'm now going to double-check against the mainline kernel to ensure there isn't something I'm missing.

    "content": null,
    "role": "assistant",
    "thought": "**Debugging a Potential NULL Pointer Dereference**

Okay, so I'm looking at commit `5e3694dafea85439313253995d43f50d32e0dd40` and immediately, my spider senses are tingling. I see `mfill_put_vma` and, *wait a minute*, there's no check for `state->vma` being NULL before the call to `uffd_mfill_unlock()`. That looks like a textbook NULL pointer dereference waiting to happen.

```c
+static void mfill_put_vma(struct mfill_state *state)
+{
+	up_read(&state->ctx->map_changing_lock);
+	uffd_mfill_unlock(state->vma);
+	state->vma = NULL;
+}
```

And I bet the crash happens right around the call to `uffd_mfill_unlock(state->vma)`. 

Now, I need to backtrace to see the possible code flow. I can see that in `mfill_get_vma`, there's a jump to `out_unlock`, but `state->vma` is *still* `NULL` because it hasn't gone through the steps to get assigned a valid value. And if `state->vma` is `NULL`, then `uffd_mfill_unlock(state->vma)` is essentially `uffd_mfill_unlock(NULL)`.

Looking at `uffd_mfill_unlock`, that's even worse. It calls `vma_end_read(vma)` or `mmap_read_unlock(vma->vm_mm)`. Either way, if `vma` is `NULL`, it's going to try to access `vma->vm_mm`, which is going to be a guaranteed crash. Or `vma_end_read(NULL)` is going to cause issues.

```c
static void uffd_mfill_unlock(struct vm_area_struct *vma)
{
	vma_end_read(vma); // OR mmap_read_unlock(vma->vm_mm)
}
```

So, I see now why a fix was implemented. It was clearly to guard against this NULL dereference. The fix would be the later addition of `if (!state->vma) return;` to prevent the crash in `mfill_put_vma`. It's a classic example of defensive programming. I'm glad someone caught that.

    "role": "assistant",
    "thought": "**Summary of Concerns Regarding `mfill_atomic` and Lock Management**

As an expert, I've identified two critical issues in the userfaultfd implementation, particularly concerning the interaction of `mfill_atomic` with VMA re-validation within the new patch. The initial bug was the lack of a check of `state->vma == NULL` in `mfill_put_vma` and the resulting lock leak when the state was returned from a failed `mfill_get_vma` call. This was addressed by Mike Rapoport, but the fix introduced a new lock leak that the patch triggers.

1.  **Vulnerability to VMA Type Change within the Loop:** The fundamental design of `mfill_atomic` assumes the VMA type remains constant throughout its execution. The new patch introduces logic that re-validates and re-acquires the VMA inside the main loop to handle VMA splits. Specifically, this happens during handling of `UFFDIO_UNREGISTER` and other scenarios. However, this re-validation process can lead to the situation where a normal, page-mapped VMA is replaced concurrently by a hugetlb VMA *during* the loop. In that event, `mfill_get_vma` will succeed, because the current code allows a hugetlb VMA to `goto out:` and thus return, but subsequent code like `mfill_atomic_pte` will then attempt to treat the hugetlb VMA as a normal, page-mapped VMA. That, in turn, will cause `mfill_atomic_install_pte` to be called with incorrect values for page table mappings, leading to page table corruption or kernel crashes because `pte_offset_map_lock` is used instead of the appropriate hugetlb functions, like `huge_pte_alloc` or `huge_ptep_get`. This is a critical vulnerability. It is also important to note that if this happens due to a split VMA, this is by design, because `mfill_put_vma` drops the necessary lock for the concurrent split to occur.
2.  **Resource Leak on Error in `mfill_get_vma`:** The second problem is a resource leak. The fix for the original null-pointer dereference introduced an issue: In several error paths inside `mfill_get_vma`, particularly where `ops = vma_uffd_ops(dst_vma)` is NULL (for example, if a VMA is unregistered concurrently), `mfill_put_vma` is called. However, because `state->vma` is only assigned to `dst_vma` upon success, it remains `NULL` in the error handling. As a result, the check `if (!state->vma)` in `mfill_put_vma` causes an immediate return, thus *leaking* both `ctx->map_changing_lock` and the VMA lock acquired by `uffd_mfill_lock()`. The new loop logic in the patch makes this resource leak far more likely to occur because it is specifically designed to handle VMA splits that trigger `UFFDIO_UNREGISTER`. Each time the VMA is split, the `mfill_get_vma` call in the loop can cause an unregistered VMA to hit the error path and leak resources. To solve this, `state->vma = dst_vma` should be set immediately after `uffd_mfill_lock(..)`, before any `goto out_unlock` statements. This ensures the lock is freed in case of an error.

    "thought": "**Analysis of userfaultfd `mfill_atomic` with VMA Splitting**

Alright, let's break this down. We're looking at how `mfill_atomic` handles VMA splits, specifically in the context of userfaultfd's `UFFDIO_COPY`. The core issue is that `mfill_atomic` initially assumes a contiguous VMA for the entire copy range, but that VMA can be split by another process while the operation is in progress. The code's new logic attempts to account for this.

The crucial function is `validate_dst_vma`, which ensures the destination range stays within a single valid VMA registered with userfaultfd. The key here is the check `dst_end > dst_vma->vm_end`.  This is where things go sideways if a VMA gets split. The initial lookup assumes a VMA covering a whole range. If `UFFDIO_UNREGISTER` or `mprotect` splits this VMA, then, `validate_dst_vma` will fail at re-validation and it will return a short copy.

The core of the patch's logic is this loop that re-validates the VMA, so if the VMA changes, we correct the offset.  The code attempts to re-acquire a valid VMA within the loop, checking `state.dst_addr < state.vma->vm_start` or `state.dst_addr >= state.vma->vm_end`. If the current VMA no longer fully encompasses the current copy target, the VMA is dropped and re-acquired.

The re-acquisition logic then sets `state.dst_start = state.dst_addr` and calculates `state.len` from the original length. Here's the key: if the VMA was split, this calculation is designed to look up the remainder of the original target. It's supposed to handle cases where we've copied part of a range and now need to deal with the part that's now in a new VMA. The re-acquired VMA MUST cover up to the original target.

The critical race condition, as the patch describes, is that the split can happen *before* we acquire the per-VMA read lock. This means we might initially get a VMA back from `lock_vma_under_rcu` which *has already* shrunk. It is this shrunk VMA that will be entered to the loop.

Because the code uses `validate_dst_vma`, it's possible for a VMA to split, even though the second VMA is still userfaultfd registered.  If the second VMA is *unregistered*, this is fine, because `validate_dst_vma` will correctly fail. But if the second VMA is still registered, then the copy continues.  If the split happened between our initial range check and our acquiring the lock, `mfill_get_vma` will return a shrunken VMA.  The loop's re-validation then correctly handles looking up the new range and if this fails, we return a short copy and let user space handle the error. This is a key safety net and works as expected.

`mfill_copy_folio_retry` is a second place where the VMA can be dropped and re-acquired within the loop. This can be due to page faults. If a split happens, the same logic will apply, we will end up with a lookup that will either succeed (if the VMA is still valid), or fail (which is by design), and return a short copy. The code is carefully set up, such that the re-acquire looks up the correct VMA and verifies it correctly.

Critically, the patch *does* handle the case where `mfill_copy_folio_retry` drops the lock and then *another* thread splits the VMA. The re-acquired VMA will be for the correct, remaining range, and `validate_dst_vma` will catch that the *original* range is not valid because of the split. Returning a short copy is the expected behavior, and is a safe outcome in all cases.

So, in summary, the patch addresses the race by re-validating the VMA within the loop and returns short copies. This is the expected and safer behavior, and is now safe even when there are concurrent VMA splits. The update of `state.dst_start` and `state.len` are done, so any retries correctly will use the last valid values. It will correctly re-validate that the range is still valid after a VMA split. When the caller, `mfill_atomic`, calls `mfill_get_vma` which in turn calls `uffd_mfill_lock` and returns the *locked* VMA! So that means, when `mfill_atomic_hugetlb` is called, `dst_vma` is *already* locked via `vma_start_read(vma)`! And crucially, `mfill_atomic_hugetlb` NEVER releases the `vma_start_read` lock until it returns! `uffd_mfill_unlock` is done only on the exit path.

Here's the problem: if `dst_vma` is a hugetlb VMA, could it get split by `UFFDIO_UNREGISTER` in the same race window? Yes, it can, and this is where it gets interesting. If `UFFDIO_UNREGISTER` splits the hugetlb VMA, `mfill_atomic_hugetlb` will iterate until `src_addr < src_start + len`. It won't revalidate `dst_addr` against `dst_vma->vm_end`! Okay, so I've been digging into this userfaultfd code, and I think I've found a critical vulnerability that was missed during a previous fix. It stems from a race condition when handling HugeTLB pages. Specifically, I see that the function `hugetlb_add_new_anon_rmap` has this critical check: `BUG_ON(address < vma->vm_start || address >= vma->vm_end)`. The problem is that `mfill_atomic_hugetlb`, which ultimately calls this function, doesn't re-validate the destination address (`dst_addr`) against the VMA's end boundary (`dst_vma->vm_end`) inside its loop.

Here's how it plays out: `mfill_atomic_hugetlb` retrieves the destination VMA (`dst_vma`) using `uffd_mfill_lock(dst_mm, dst_start, len)`. Now, if a `UFFDIO_UNREGISTER` operation happens concurrently, and the VMA is split *within* the race window, the `dst_vma` returned from `uffd_mfill_lock` is already shrunk. The loop then proceeds, thinking it's still operating within the original VMA boundaries. In the first iteration, it likely works. However, the next iteration will increment `dst_addr`, which will now be *beyond* the *new*, shrunk VMA's end. This is where `hugetlb_mfill_atomic_pte` is invoked, which then calls `hugetlb_add_new_anon_rmap`, triggering that `BUG_ON` and crashing the kernel.

I've confirmed that this is essentially the same bug that was patched in the regular `mfill_atomic` function, but somehow, `mfill_atomic_hugetlb` was overlooked. The original patch only modified `mfill_atomic`, and the crucial validation step was not added to its HugeTLB counterpart! I can see that `is_vm_hugetlb_page(state.vma)` is true for HugeTLB VMAs so it's directly calling `mfill_atomic_hugetlb`.

The key here is that `UFFDIO_UNREGISTER` is allowed on HugeTLB VMAs; I verified that userfaultfd supports HugeTLB, and HugeTLB VMAs *can* be split (by `UFFDIO_UNREGISTER` or `mprotect`).  And the race conditions using `lock_vma_under_rcu` are identical.

To be extra sure, I checked `hugetlb_add_new_anon_rmap` is called when private hugetlb mappings via `UFFDIO_COPY` with `hugetlb_mfill_atomic_pte`.  It clearly is, because it's handling the non-pagecache case, where the `folio` is not in the pagecache. So, this isn't just a theoretical issue; it's a real vulnerability.

I need to quickly assess the impact here.  Are there any other functions that get a VMA using a locking mechanism and then iterate without re-validating the VMA?  I am looking now to see whether `move_pages` is vulnerable...

    "thought": "**Vulnerability Analysis of `move_pages` and Potential Race Conditions**

Okay, so I'm diving into the `move_pages()` function, specifically its interaction with VMAs and potential race conditions. It looks like the core of the problem stems from how `uffd_move_lock` returns `dst_vma` and `src_vma`. The loop iterates based on the initial `src_start`, `dst_start`, and `len` passed to `move_pages()`. Within the loop, it allocates `src_pmd` and `dst_pmd`. The crucial question is: if either the source or destination VMA is *split* during the process, is there any re-validation of `src_addr` against `src_vma`'s bounds or `dst_addr` against `dst_vma`'s bounds within the loop?

Looking deeper, `move_pages` calls `move_pages_huge_pmd` and `move_pages_ptes`, and I'm wondering if either of those routines has bounds checks that would protect against out-of-bounds access.  I'm particularly concerned about the implications if `move_pages_ptes` is called with `dst_vma` but `dst_addr` falls outside the valid range. It's possible we could end up mapping pages to the wrong VMA, or trigger other checks. The other problem is that `uffd_move_lock` uses `lock_vma_under_rcu` for both `src_vma` and `dst_vma`. This implies that *either* VMA can be shrunk.

Aha! Here's the critical observation: The loop in `move_pages` doesn't re-validate the VMA bounds! It iterates based only on the initial `len` and the starting addresses. Specifically:

```c
	for (src_addr = src_start, dst_addr = dst_start, src_end = src_start + len;
	     src_addr < src_end;) {
...
		/* Proceed to the next page */
		dst_addr += step_size;
		src_addr += step_size;
		moved += step_size;
	}
```

There's no check to see if `dst_addr` is still within `dst_vma->vm_end` or `src_addr` is within `src_vma->vm_end` *inside* the loop. This is the exact same race condition the bug report describes! The loop iterates up to `len` without verifying that the destination or source addresses are still valid, allowing for the shrunk VMA to cause the loop to read and write outside its boundaries. The bug report is spot-on: The VMA is shrunk after the initial check but before the subsequent loop operations. On the next iteration, and this is what triggers the vulnerability, the `mfill_atomic_install_pte` could call `folio_add_new_anon_rmap` with `dst_addr` outside the VMA, thus triggering a sanity check.

Now, I'm thinking about the implications. `move_pages` calls `move_pages_ptes`. If the PTE is moved and updated, it is likely that the rmap is also updated. It does *not* do so because it just moves the PTEs, but it *does* pass `dst_vma` and `src_vma` to `move_pages_ptes`, so whatever *that* calls, could be relevant. So, my gut feeling is that we're talking about a race condition in the page table manipulation, and that it's possible for `move_pages_ptes` to be called with addresses that are outside of a VMA, or at least no longer within the valid VMA region. The bug is the same; the VMA shrunk after the initial setup. I need to keep looking to see if there are any checks of bounds within the called functions.

    "thought": "**My Concerns Regarding Page Table Entry Manipulation**

Okay, so I'm looking at `move_pages_ptes` and it's dealing with Present Page Table Entries (PTEs). First, it checks if the source PTE is present. If it is and it points to a zero page, it branches off to `move_zeropage_pte`, which is fine, I know how that works. But then, if it's not a zero page, it gets the folio. And then, it calls `move_present_ptes`. That's where I start getting a little concerned.

The question is, does `move_present_ptes` *validate* that the destination address (`dst_addr`) actually *lies within* the destination VMA (`dst_vma`)? My understanding is, if `UFFDIO_UNREGISTER` has been called, it's possible the VMA has been split or shrunk. If that happens, then `dst_addr` could very well be *outside* the bounds of the new `dst_vma`. That's a problem! We could be mapping pages to an invalid address, which could result in a kernel panic!

And *then* it calls `move_present_ptes` which *again* takes the `dst_vma` and `dst_addr` as input! Specifically, am I correct in thinking `move_present_ptes` calls `folio_move_anon_rmap`? Because `folio_move_anon_rmap` also uses both `dst_vma` and `dst_addr`. This smells like a vulnerability if it doesn't validate properly. So the question is: where is the validity check? I really need to double-check the logic within `move_present_ptes` itself, and especially `folio_move_anon_rmap` to be certain!

    "thought": "**Critical Vulnerabilities Discovered in `move_pages` and Potential Memory Leaks!**

Okay, so I've been digging into the `move_pages` code, and I've got a major problem on my hands. It started with the call to `folio_move_anon_rmap` within `move_present_ptes()`. I noticed that while `folio_move_anon_rmap` does check for `anon_vma` validity, there's no boundary check when setting the PTEs with `set_pte_at()`. That means we're potentially mapping pages outside the destination VMA (`dst_vma`)!

This is a **huge** issue. If `dst_addr` goes beyond `dst_vma->vm_end`, the page gets mapped, but the VMA doesn't encompass it. This creates a situation where userspace can access that rogue page if another VMA covers it, or even worse, it could just sit in the page table, leaking memory after the process exits. `unmap_vmas` only works within VMA boundaries, so those out-of-bounds pages are *guaranteed* to leak!

Then, I went deeper. The loop within `move_pages` itself has no validation check! The code iterates:

```c
	for (src_addr = src_start, dst_addr = dst_start, src_end = src_start + len;
	     src_addr < src_end;) {
```

Critically, there's no check inside this loop to see if `src_addr` is still within `src_vma->vm_end`.  And we're using `lock_vma_under_rcu` for *both* the source and destination VMAs, meaning **both** can be shrunk concurrently due to UFFDIO_UNREGISTER or mprotect operations, right? This is a complete disaster with `CONFIG_PER_VMA_LOCK` enabled!

Essentially, if a UFFDIO_UNREGISTER or mprotect shrinks the VMAs while `move_pages` is running, the loop will happily keep moving pages, now from/to *outside* the intended VMAs. This is a severe security hole; we're talking about leaking pages to other VMAs and potentially stealing pages.

The author of the previous fix only caught the issue in `mfill_atomic`, but completely missed the *two* other vulnerable loops: `mfill_atomic_hugetlb()` and, critically, `move_pages()`!

Let's do a sweep of the codebase for similar issues. `mwriteprotect_range()` uses `mmap_read_lock()` and `for_each_vma_range`, so it's safe. `userfaultfd_register_range` and `userfaultfd_release_all` both also hold the `mmap_write_lock`, so they are safe as well.

The common factor seems to be functions that use `lock_vma_under_rcu` and loop over memory ranges, so, checking the code, the vulnerable functions are called from:

*   `uffd_mfill_lock` is used by:
    *   `mfill_get_vma` (which calls `mfill_atomic` and `mfill_copy_folio_retry`)

Okay, here's what I'm seeing. `uffd_mfill_lock` and `uffd_move_lock` are the only functions that call `uffd_lock_vma`, which in turn calls `lock_vma_under_rcu`. Both of these functions operate within a specific range, defined by `len`, assuming the destination or source VMA encompasses that full range. And that encompassing was *confirmed* before a potential split race.

The key point here is `validate_dst_vma`. This function checks if the returned VMA's `vm_end` covers the original, full range. However, here's the race condition: If a VMA's start is read using `vma_start_read`, and that VMA is *shrinking* before the read lock is completely acquired (because `vma_start_write` doesn't wait for readers, since the refcount isn't incremented yet), `validate_dst_vma` will see the *shrunken* `vm_end`.

Let's break down `uffd_mfill_lock` step by step:
```c
static struct vm_area_struct *uffd_mfill_lock(struct mm_struct *dst_mm,
					      unsigned long dst_start,
					      unsigned long len)
{
	struct vm_area_struct *dst_vma;

	dst_vma = uffd_lock_vma(dst_mm, dst_start);
	if (IS_ERR(dst_vma) || validate_dst_vma(dst_vma, dst_start + len))
		return dst_vma;

	vma_end_read(dst_vma);
	return ERR_PTR(-ENOENT);
}
```
Crucially, `validate_dst_vma` checks `dst_vma->vm_end >= dst_start + len`. If the VMA has already been split *before* `validate_dst_vma` executes, `dst_vma->vm_end` *will* be smaller. Thus, `validate_dst_vma` should return `false`, and `uffd_mfill_lock` should return `-ENOENT`.

During this window, `vm_refcnt` is at its base value, so `vma_start_write()` proceeds without waiting, which leads to the VMA shrinking in place. The sequence number checks in `vma_start_read` will miss this because the `mm_lock_seq` has been incremented past `vm_lock_seq` *after* the `mmap_write_unlock()`, so the checks will be unequal and return the split VMA.

Aha! `validate_dst_vma` runs *after* `vma_start_read` completes! If the VMA is split *during* `vma_start_read`, the write lock is taken, `vm_end` shrinks, and then the write lock is released. Then `vma_start_read` returns *true*! And *then* `uffd_mfill_lock` calls `validate_dst_vma(dst_vma, dst_start + len)`. Because `vm_end` has shrunk, `dst_vma->vm_end` will be less than `dst_start + len`. Therefore `validate_dst_vma` should return *false*.

So, if `validate_dst_vma` returns false, `uffd_mfill_lock` should drop the read lock and return `-ENOENT`. Then `mfill_atomic` should simply exit, right? Why did a sanity check trigger in `mfill_atomic_install_pte` then?

    "thought": "**Unraveling the Concurrency Bug in UFFD mfill_atomic**

Okay, here's my thought process on this intriguing concurrency issue. It seems we're dealing with a subtle race condition in the userfaultfd implementation, specifically around VMA splitting during `mfill_atomic`. The core problem appears to be that a VMA can be shrunk *under* us, due to concurrent unregister operations.

My initial thought was that `validate_dst_vma` should catch the shrunken VMA and prevent the issue. Indeed, it *does* check `dst_vma->vm_end >= dst_start + len`. If a split causes `dst_vma->vm_end` to shrink below what we expect, then `validate_dst_vma` should return false, leading to an immediate error (`-ENOENT`) and early exit. But the bug is happening, so that means this isn't the whole story. The challenge is, how does this bypass the check?

Let's break down the likely sequence, since the error indicates that `dst_addr >= vm_end`. The patch points to a race window *before* `vm_refcnt++` inside `vma_start_read`. This is where a `vma_start_write` can get a head start, so to speak. If this writer shrinks `vm_end` *before* our reader increments `vm_refcnt`, it can modify the VMA under us. This is where seqnum checks come in. Because the writer is using `mmap_write_unlock()` to shrink the VMA, this triggers the increment of the `mm->mm_lock_seq` variable. When `vma_start_read` executes its seqnum check *after* `mmap_write_unlock()`, it finds that `mm_lock_seq` is larger than `vm_lock_seq`. This causes `vma_start_read` to erroneously conclude that the VMA is *not* write-locked, so it returns the shrunken VMA.

This is where the magic happens! This split VMA somehow slips through. If the VMA is already split BEFORE `validate_dst_vma` is run, then `validate_dst_vma` is called *after* this happens. Then `validate_dst_vma` would see the *shrunken* VMA, the check would fail, and we'd correctly exit. The problem is with the *timing* of the split relative to our checks.

The key insight is the "race window." The `__split_vma` function can begin the split and shrink `vm_end` *before* our reader, which calls `validate_dst_vma` at the start of the `uffd_mfill_lock` function. In this case, `validate_dst_vma` is using a stale `vm_end` value from BEFORE the split. This makes the check *succeed*, because the split happens *after* `validate_dst_vma` passes, and the loop is able to begin!

The split happened between the check in `uffd_mfill_lock` and the iterations inside the loop. The `mfill_copy_folio_retry` function in the original code is responsible for dropping the lock, copying from user space and calling `mfill_get_vma` function again to revalidate the VMA, which is where the race condition triggers the error.

Let me think. The critical piece seems to be that a writer can bypass our reader in this window. Then `validate_dst_vma` will read the shrunken `vm_end` value, but `dst_addr` in the loop can surpass `vm_end`, due to the split. The original code did not account for this possibility. The fix is to add validation inside the loop. This ensures that a concurrent split is caught before we try to process an invalid VMA.

Essentially, the bug boils down to a race between the reader and a concurrent writer (a UFFDIO_UNREGISTER operation) that modifies the VMA. The old code lacked the protection needed to detect and handle this concurrent modification.

This race bypasses standard locking mechanisms due to a specific sequence number-related issue, allowing a writer to shrink the VMA concurrently with a reader iterating through it.

An attacker can concurrently shrink and concurrently steal and map to their own memory region, even read-only memory, by stealing pages that fall into another VMA, especially if it's a privileged or restricted VMA.

The core of the problem lies in the `mfill_atomic` function. This function uses a loop that iterates through the destination address space, copying data page by page. Within this loop, `validate_dst_vma` is the guardrail: it makes sure that each destination address is within the bounds of the current VMA. A VMA is essentially a memory region, and my job is to make sure the copy happens within the region. If a VMA is split during the process, it means we've essentially created new VMAs by breaking the region up.

The standard case works wonderfully. Let's say we start with VMA `[0x1000, 0x3000)` and want to copy it. The loop progresses, updating `dst_addr`.  If the VMA is split, say into `[0x1000, 0x2000)` and `[0x2000, 0x3000)`, the initial VMA's `vm_end` shrinks to `0x2000`. The code revalidates the VMA on each iteration of the loop. The complexity arises when a split occurs, not at the loop iteration, but during the page-copying phase itself. This is where `mfill_copy_folio_retry` comes into play. If, during a `copy_from_user` operation (which can block), a `UFFDIO_UNREGISTER` operation, or an `mprotect` operation, causes a split.

static inline pgoff_t vma_hugecache_offset(struct hstate *h,

I then reviewed the original patch and noticed the race window is between the seqnum checks and `vm_refcnt` increment. It stated that because of this race condition, the `vm_end` of the VMA can be shrunk in place. The patch addressed a similar bug in `mfill_atomic` with a check to re-validate `dst_addr` against VMA bounds on each iteration. `mfill_atomic` had a `WARNING` not a `BUG_ON`, so it's a denial of service (DoS) vulnerability.

I missed something! The patch checks the end of the current `vma` and updates the values if the current `dst_addr` is not in the current `vma`. It updates state.dst_start with state.dst_addr, and state.len as the difference. *Aha!* So the loop *continues* because the VMA can shrink, we update the `state` struct, and get a new VMA! Now the loop *does* continue. If `vma->vm_end` shrinks *inside* the loop while we are holding the read lock, `vma_start_write` *should* be blocking. But it is happening *before*. Therefore, it is possible for `state.dst_addr >= state.vma->vm_end`.

This means the copy can begin even if the copy is larger than any one VMA (or even a series of contiguous VMAs) or if it goes past the end of the VMA!  While the patch adds a check inside the loop, this bypasses the initial validation, and opens a race window, and changes the semantics of this call. **Finding 2: Lock leak in `mfill_get_vma()` error paths due to NULL `state->vma`.**

The patch introduces a mid-loop re-validation of the VMA and calls `mfill_put_vma(&state)` followed by `mfill_get_vma(&state)`. The function `mfill_put_vma` correctly sets `state.vma` to `NULL`. This means the `map_changing_lock` and the `VMA` lock are leaked, because the error path exits without unlocking them! This is a textbook lock leak.

*   **Severity: High** - This lock leak can cause a full system hang.

**Finding 3: Type Confusion leading to page table corruption when re-acquired VMA is hugetlb.**

Before the loop, `mfill_atomic` correctly checks if the VMA is a hugetlb VMA and uses `mfill_atomic_hugetlb`.     mm/userfaultfd: re-validate vma in mfill_atomic() loop under CONFIG_PER_VMA_LOCK

The commit adds a mid-loop VMA bounds check to mfill_atomic() to handle
concurrent UFFDIO_UNREGISTER splits. It re-acquires the VMA if the current
address falls outside the split VMA's bounds.

Reported-by: syzbot+e24a2e34fad0efbac047@syzkaller.appspotmail.com

> diff --git a/mm/userfaultfd.c b/mm/userfaultfd.c

> @@ -910,6 +910,22 @@ static __always_inline ssize_t mfill_atomic(struct userfaultfd_ctx *ctx,
>

>  		VM_WARN_ON_ONCE(state.dst_addr >= dst_start + len);
> +	       /*
> +		* Under CONFIG_PER_VMA_LOCK, a concurrent UFFDIO_UNREGISTER can
> +		* split state.vma while we hold only the per-VMA read lock. The
> +		* split shrinks vma->vm_end in place, causing dst_addr to fall
> +		* outside the VMA bounds. Re-validate dst_addr on each iteration

> +			state.len = dst_start + len - state.dst_addr;

Does the omission of the .len field in the initial designated initializer
for state cause state.len to be initialized to 0 at the start of
mfill_atomic()?

If so, the first call to mfill_get_vma(&state) outside this loop passes
state.len (0) to uffd_mfill_lock(), bypassing the requirement that the
entire requested range fits within the VMA. While the new mid-loop check
masks the resulting out-of-bounds access, it appears cross-VMA operations
are improperly allowed to begin processing.

> +			err = mfill_get_vma(&state);

Can this lead to a lock leak on error paths?

mfill_put_vma(&state) sets state.vma = NULL before this call. Inside
mfill_get_vma(), a new dst_vma is locked and map_changing_lock is acquired.

If validation fails (for example, !ops because the VMA was split by
UFFDIO_UNREGISTER), mfill_get_vma() jumps to out_unlock and calls
mfill_put_vma(state).

Since state->vma is only updated to dst_vma on success at the out label,
it is still NULL during the error path. mfill_put_vma() will immediately
return, failing to release map_changing_lock and unlock the VMA.

Can this also lead to page table corruption if the re-acquired VMA is a
hugetlb VMA?

mfill_get_vma() allows hugetlb VMAs, returning 0 and skipping ops checks
via the is_vm_hugetlb_page(dst_vma) branch.

If a concurrent thread replaces the normal VMA at dst_addr with a hugetlb
VMA, mfill_get_vma() will succeed and update state.vma. The loop will then
continue and call mfill_get_pmd() and mfill_atomic_pte(), which allocate
standard PMDs and PTEs for a hugetlb mapping.

> +			if (err)
> +				break;
> +		}
>  
>  		err = mfill_get_pmd(&state);

Does mfill_atomic_hugetlb() require a similar fix?

It holds the same uffd_mfill_lock() across its loop but lacks this new
in-loop bounds re-validation check against dst_vma->vm_end.

If the hugetlb VMA shrinks concurrently via UFFDIO_UNREGISTER, dst_addr
could exceed the VMA bounds, causing hugetlb_mfill_atomic_pte() to be
called with an out-of-bounds address.
